items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Cultural control is one of the principal pillars of integrated management of Tuta absoluta because it targets the environment in which the pest lives and the sources that sustain it, rather than relying solely on killing individuals present on plants at the time of treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control is one of the principal pillars of integrated management of Tuta absoluta because it targets the environment in which the pest lives and the sources that sustain it, rather than relying solely on killing individuals present on plants at the time of treatment.

items.text: Cultural control includes all practices related to field selection, crop rotation, planting dates, sanitation, management of alternative hosts, irrigation, fertilization, plant density, harvesting, and disposal of crop residues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control includes all practices related to field selection, crop rotation, planting dates, sanitation, management of [[term:alternative-host]]alternative hosts[[/term]], irrigation, fertilization, plant density, harvesting, and disposal of crop residues.

items.text: These practices generally do not function as rapid eradication methods. Instead, they act to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These practices generally do not function as rapid [[term:eradication]]eradication[[/term]] methods. Instead, they act to:

items: Reduce the number of generations the pest can complete.
Prevent persistence of the green bridge.
Improve the plant’s ability to tolerate injury.
Facilitate monitoring and penetration of control measures.
Reduce the need for chemical sprays.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduce the number of generations the pest can complete.
[[prepared]]Improve the plant’s ability to tolerate injury.
[[prepared]]Facilitate monitoring and penetration of control measures.

items.text: Cultural control affects more than one pest stage, and most of its components can be implemented before infestation reaches a high level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control affects more than one pest stage, and most of its components can be implemented before infestation reaches a high level.

items.text: However, it usually does not provide complete control when used alone, especially in areas with overlapping planting seasons and continuous immigration sources. It must therefore be integrated with monitoring and biological, behavioral, and rational chemical control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, it usually does not provide complete control when used alone, especially in areas with overlapping planting seasons and continuous immigration sources. It must therefore be integrated with monitoring and biological, behavioral, and rational chemical control.

items.text: Objectives of Cultural Control
Cultural control aims to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control aims to:

items: 2. Reduce sources of eggs, larvae, and pupae.
3. Prevent old fields from producing moths that migrate into new crops.
4. Remove volunteer tomato plants and important alternative hosts.
6. Reduce field suitability for pest reproduction.
7. Produce a balanced plant capable of tolerating limited infestation.
8. Avoid excessive vegetative growth that hinders inspection and coverage.
9. Facilitate early detection of infestation foci.
10. Maintain canopy cover that protects fruits from sunlight.
11. Prevent accumulation of infested fruits and crop residues.
12. Reduce pest pressure at the production-area level.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. Reduce sources of eggs, larvae, and pupae.
[[prepared]]3. Prevent old fields from producing moths that migrate into new crops.
[[prepared]]4. Remove volunteer tomato plants and important alternative hosts.
[[prepared]]6. Reduce field suitability for pest reproduction.
[[prepared]]7. Produce a balanced plant capable of tolerating limited infestation.
[[prepared]]8. Avoid excessive vegetative growth that hinders inspection and coverage.
[[prepared]]9. Facilitate early detection of infestation foci.
[[prepared]]10. Maintain canopy cover that protects fruits from sunlight.
[[prepared]]11. Prevent accumulation of infested fruits and crop residues.
[[prepared]]12. Reduce pest pressure at the production-area level.

items.text: Position of Cultural Control Within the IPM Program
Cultural control begins before planting and continues until after harvest ends.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control begins before planting and continues until after harvest ends.

items.text: It can be divided chronologically into:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It can be divided chronologically into:

items.text: Before Planting

items: Disposal of residues from the previous crop.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Disposal of residues from the previous crop.

items.text: During the Season

items: Removal of infestation foci and infested plant parts when appropriate.
Prevention of accumulation of infested fruits.
Monitoring of alternative hosts and neighboring fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Removal of infestation foci and infested plant parts when appropriate.
[[prepared]]Monitoring of alternative hosts and neighboring fields.

items.text: After Harvest

items: Termination of the crop at the appropriate time.
Collection of fruits left in the field.
Prevention of a new generation after production has stopped.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Termination of the crop at the appropriate time.
[[prepared]]Collection of fruits left in the field.
[[prepared]]Prevention of a new generation after production has stopped.

items.text: Crop Rotation
Crop rotation means avoiding continuous planting of tomato or suitable hosts of Tuta absoluta in the same field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop rotation means avoiding continuous planting of tomato or suitable hosts of Tuta absoluta in the same field.

items.text: Rotation is more effective when tomato is followed by a non-solanaceous crop on which the pest cannot complete its life cycle.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rotation is more effective when tomato is followed by a non-solanaceous crop on which the pest cannot complete its life cycle.

items.text: Examples of crops that may be included in the rotation, according to region and production system, include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examples of crops that may be included in the rotation, according to region and production system, include:

items: Other crops locally confirmed not to be complete hosts of the pest.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Other crops locally confirmed not to be complete hosts of the pest.

items.text: Changing only the cultivar or shifting from processing tomatoes to fresh-market tomatoes is insufficient because both belong to the same plant species and support the pest life cycle.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Changing only the cultivar or shifting from processing tomatoes to fresh-market tomatoes is insufficient because both belong to the same plant species and support the pest life cycle.

items.text: Crops That Do Not Adequately Break the Cycle
Planting the following solanaceous crops immediately after tomato does not fully break the pest cycle:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Planting the following solanaceous crops immediately after tomato does not fully break the pest cycle:

items: Some cultivated or wild Solanum species.

items.text: The pest can complete its life cycle on several of these plants, although they may be less suitable than tomato.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest can complete its life cycle on several of these plants, although they may be less suitable than tomato.

items.text: Pepper is generally far less suitable and results are inconsistent among studies. Nevertheless, it should not be relied upon alone as a guaranteed barrier in a heavily infested area without field monitoring.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pepper is generally far less suitable and results are inconsistent among studies. Nevertheless, it should not be relied upon alone as a guaranteed barrier in a heavily infested area without field monitoring.

items.text: Conditions Required for Successful Crop Rotation
Crop rotation fails to reduce the pest when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop rotation fails to reduce the pest when:

items: Tomato residues remain in the field.
Black nightshade persists along field edges.
Old tomato fields remain immediately adjacent.
Infested greenhouses or nurseries persist in the area.
Infested transplants are moved into the next crop.
A suitable solanaceous crop is planted directly afterward.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Infested greenhouses or nurseries persist in the area.
[[prepared]]Infested transplants are moved into the next crop.
[[prepared]]A suitable solanaceous crop is planted directly afterward.

items.text: An effective rotation therefore consists of three interrelated elements:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An effective rotation therefore consists of three interrelated elements:

items: 3. A period during which the pest cannot reproduce successfully.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]3. A period during which the pest cannot reproduce successfully.

items.text: Host-Free Period
A host-free period is one of the strongest cultural measures at the area-wide level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A host-free period is one of the strongest cultural measures at the area-wide level.

items.text: It means the absence of plants capable of supporting the pest life cycle, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It means the absence of plants capable of supporting the pest life cycle, including:

items.text: A host-free period helps to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A host-free period helps to:

items: Reduce moth numbers before the next crop.
Break the green bridge between planting seasons.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduce moth numbers before the next crop.
[[prepared]]Break the green bridge between planting seasons.

items.text: No single fixed duration is suitable for every region because success is affected by temperature, pupal survival, wild hosts, and overlapping greenhouses and nurseries.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single fixed duration is suitable for every region because success is affected by temperature, pupal survival, wild hosts, and overlapping greenhouses and nurseries.

items.text: The period loses its value when commercial tomato is absent but volunteer tomato plants or host weeds remain.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The period loses its value when commercial tomato is absent but volunteer tomato plants or host weeds remain.

items.text: Organization of Crop Succession
Crop succession should be considered at the production-area level rather than only at the individual-field level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop succession should be considered at the production-area level rather than only at the individual-field level.

items.text: A tomato crop may end in one field while neighboring fields contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A tomato crop may end in one field while neighboring fields contain:

items: Transplants for a new planting season.
An old field still bearing a limited number of fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]An old field still bearing a limited number of fruits.

items.text: This overlap permits continuous movement of the pest from one host to another.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This overlap permits continuous movement of the pest from one host to another.

items.text: The following should preferably be coordinated:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should preferably be coordinated:

items: Dates on which old crops end.
Transplanting date for the next crop.

items.text: Planting Dates
Planting date can affect crop exposure to Tuta absoluta through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Planting date can affect crop exposure to Tuta absoluta through:

items: Number of generations possible during the season.
Length of time the crop remains in the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Number of generations possible during the season.
[[prepared]]Length of time the crop remains in the field.

items.text: There is no universal planting date that prevents infestation because pest abundance and climate differ among regions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no universal planting date that prevents infestation because pest abundance and climate differ among regions.

items.text: The best date should be determined locally using:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The best date should be determined locally using:

items: Historical timing of pest arrival in the area.
Dates of removal of old crops.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Historical timing of pest arrival in the area.

items.text: Early or Late Planting
Early planting may provide an advantage in an area where pest numbers rise late, allowing plants to reach advanced stages before infestation peaks.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early planting may provide an advantage in an area where pest numbers rise late, allowing plants to reach advanced stages before infestation peaks.

items.text: However, early planting may be more hazardous when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, early planting may be more hazardous when:

items: It coincides with heavily infested old fields.
A long growing season allows more generations to develop.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]It coincides with heavily infested old fields.
[[prepared]]A long growing season allows more generations to develop.

items.text: Late planting may be exposed to heavy immigration from previous fields where control has stopped.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Late planting may be exposed to heavy immigration from previous fields where control has stopped.

items.text: The decision to plant early or late should therefore be based on temporal analysis of trap data and surrounding planting seasons, rather than a general rule.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision to plant early or late should therefore be based on temporal analysis of trap data and surrounding planting seasons, rather than a general rule.

items.text: Cultivar Cycle Length
The longer a cultivar remains in the field, the longer it is exposed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The longer a cultivar remains in the field, the longer it is exposed to:

items.text: Short-cycle cultivars may be exposed to fewer generations under some conditions, but cultivar selection does not depend on Tuta alone. It also depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Short-cycle cultivars may be exposed to fewer generations under some conditions, but cultivar selection does not depend on Tuta alone. It also depends on:

items.text: No commercial cultivar is completely immune to the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No commercial cultivar is completely immune to the pest.

items.text: Field-Site Selection
When alternatives are available, new tomato fields should preferably not be established beside:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When alternatives are available, new tomato fields should preferably not be established beside:

items: A heavily infested old tomato field.
A field in which spraying has stopped.
A sorting station discarding damaged fruits.
Large areas of volunteer tomato plants.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A field in which spraying has stopped.

items.text: A map of risk sources should be used to identify the most exposed field edges, distribute traps, and intensify inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A map of risk sources should be used to identify the most exposed field edges, distribute traps, and intensify inspection.

items.text: Distance alone does not prevent immigration, but it may reduce pressure when integrated with source management.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Distance alone does not prevent immigration, but it may reduce pressure when integrated with source management.

items.text: Agricultural Sanitation During the Season
Agricultural sanitation means removing tissues and sources containing living pest stages, not merely making the field appear visually clean.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Agricultural sanitation means removing tissues and sources containing living pest stages, not merely making the field appear visually clean.

items.text: It includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It includes:

items: Collection of severely infested growing points.
Removal of infested and fallen fruits.
Prevention of accumulation of infested tomato leaves.
Preventing rejected fruits from being discarded between rows.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevention of accumulation of infested tomato leaves.
[[prepared]]Preventing rejected fruits from being discarded between rows.

items.text: Sanitation must be implemented in a manner that does not transfer infestation elsewhere.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sanitation must be implemented in a manner that does not transfer infestation elsewhere.

items.text: Removal of Infested Leaves
A limited number of heavily infested leaves may be removed when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A limited number of heavily infested leaves may be removed when:

items: Leaves can be collected without scattering them.
Removal will not eliminate a large leaf area.
Leaves are collected in a closed container.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Leaves can be collected without scattering them.
[[prepared]]Removal will not eliminate a large leaf area.
[[prepared]]Leaves are collected in a closed container.

items.text: Removing every leaf bearing a small mine is not recommended because it may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Removing every leaf bearing a small mine is not recommended because it may cause:

items.text: Removal is more rational at the beginning of a focus and less practical after infestation spreads throughout the field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Removal is more rational at the beginning of a focus and less practical after infestation spreads throughout the field.

items.text: Removal of Infested Growing Points and Buds
An infested growing point may be removed when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An infested growing point may be removed when:

items: It contains a larva or obvious frass.

items.text: However, growing-point removal is not suitable for every determinate processing-tomato system because it may affect cluster number and plant architecture.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, growing-point removal is not suitable for every determinate processing-tomato system because it may affect cluster number and plant architecture.

items.text: The operation should not become indiscriminate broad pruning.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The operation should not become indiscriminate broad pruning.

items.text: Collection of Infested Fruits
Fruits should be collected when they have:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruits should be collected when they have:

items.text: Collection is important because an infested fruit may contain a larva capable of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collection is important because an infested fruit may contain a larva capable of:

items.text: Rejected fruits should not be left in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rejected fruits should not be left in:

items.text: Handling Removed Plant Material
Cutting an infested plant part and dropping it on the soil is insufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cutting an infested plant part and dropping it on the soil is insufficient.

items.text: Possible disposal methods include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Possible disposal methods include:

items: Collection in sealed bags or containers.
Fine shredding followed by a treatment that prevents survival of pest stages.
Burial at a suitable depth where regulations and soil conditions permit.
Thermophilic composting that reaches temperatures sufficient to kill the pest.
Tight covering and exposure to high temperatures until decomposition occurs.
Transport to a treatment site away from tomato fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Fine shredding followed by a treatment that prevents survival of pest stages.
[[prepared]]Burial at a suitable depth where regulations and soil conditions permit.
[[prepared]]Thermophilic composting that reaches temperatures sufficient to kill the pest.
[[prepared]]Tight covering and exposure to high temperatures until decomposition occurs.
[[prepared]]Transport to a treatment site away from tomato fields.

items.text: Open burning is not recommended as a general practice because of pollution and fire risk. It should be used only where officially permitted and under safety controls.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Open burning is not recommended as a general practice because of pollution and fire risk. It should be used only where officially permitted and under safety controls.

items.text: Volunteer Tomato Plants
Volunteer tomatoes are among the most dangerous components of the green bridge because they are the principal host itself.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Volunteer tomatoes are among the most dangerous components of the green bridge because they are the principal host itself.

items.text: They originate from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They originate from:

items.text: They should be removed from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should be removed from:

items.text: They should preferably be removed before producing sufficient vegetative growth to support completion of the pest life cycle.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should preferably be removed before producing sufficient vegetative growth to support completion of the pest life cycle.

items.text: Management of Host Weeds
Management should focus on species demonstrated to support the complete pest life cycle, particularly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management should focus on species demonstrated to support the complete pest life cycle, particularly:

items: Solanum coagulans and related species where present.
Wild solanaceous species actually carrying infestation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Solanum coagulans and related species where present.

items.text: All weeds should not be treated as equally suitable hosts.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]All weeds should not be treated as equally suitable hosts.

items.text: A distinction should be made among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction should be made among:

items: A plant supporting only limited experimental infestation.
A plant on which only a moth was recorded.
A non-host plant that benefits natural enemies.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A plant supporting only limited experimental infestation.
[[prepared]]A plant on which only a moth was recorded.
[[prepared]]A non-[[term:host-plant]]host plant[[/term]] that benefits natural enemies.

items.text: Timing of Weed Control
Large, infested solanaceous host populations should be prevented from developing near the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Large, infested solanaceous host populations should be prevented from developing near the crop.

items.text: However, sudden removal of a large infested plant mass may drive adult moths toward recently planted tomato.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, sudden removal of a large infested plant mass may drive adult moths toward recently planted tomato.

items.text: When removing a major source:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When removing a major source:

items: Plants should be collected or shredded immediately.
They should not be left wilting along the edge.
Traps and inspections should be intensified in the new field.
Timing should be coordinated with neighboring growers.
Plants should be removed before infestation reaches high density whenever possible.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Plants should be collected or shredded immediately.
[[prepared]]They should not be left wilting along the edge.
[[prepared]]Traps and inspections should be intensified in the new field.
[[prepared]]Timing should be coordinated with neighboring growers.
[[prepared]]Plants should be removed before infestation reaches high density whenever possible.

items.text: Conservation of Beneficial Non-Host Weeds
Cultural control does not mean converting the production system into land completely devoid of plants other than tomato.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control does not mean converting the production system into land completely devoid of plants other than tomato.

items.text: Some non-host plants may provide:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some non-host plants may provide:

items: Ecological diversity that improves natural control.

items.text: However, they should be confirmed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, they should be confirmed to:

items: Not be a proven Tuta host.
Not compete severely with the crop.
Not harbor other serious pests or diseases.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Not harbor other serious pests or diseases.

items.text: The decision should be based on plant identity and function, rather than the general label “weed.”
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should be based on plant identity and function, rather than the general label “weed.”

items.text: Management of Old Fields
An old field retained after economic returns have declined represents a major risk, particularly when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An old field retained after economic returns have declined represents a major risk, particularly when:

items.text: Such fields produce increasing numbers of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such fields produce increasing numbers of:

items.text: A clear decision is required:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A clear decision is required:

items: Either continue managing the crop during the economically productive harvest period.
Or terminate the crop and remove plants rapidly.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Either continue managing the crop during the economically productive harvest period.
[[prepared]]Or terminate the crop and remove plants rapidly.

items.text: Leaving a live field without economically worthwhile harvest or control is one of the most serious area-wide errors.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaving a live field without economically worthwhile harvest or control is one of the most serious area-wide errors.

items.text: Final Harvest Pickings
Growers may attempt to retain a field for a limited number of additional harvests, while the value of these pickings becomes lower than the damage caused by the field to the surrounding area.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Growers may attempt to retain a field for a limited number of additional harvests, while the value of these pickings becomes lower than the damage caused by the field to the surrounding area.

items.text: The decision should be evaluated according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should be evaluated according to:

items: Ability of the field to produce new moths.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability of the field to produce new moths.

items.text: In contracted projects or clustered production areas, the impact of the field on neighboring growers should be included in the decision, not only the profit of the individual field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In contracted projects or clustered production areas, the impact of the field on neighboring growers should be included in the decision, not only the profit of the individual field.

items.text: Removal of Crop Residues After Harvest
Crop residues may contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop residues may contain:

items: Larvae inside growing points and stems.
Pupae between leaves or on the soil surface.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Pupae between leaves or on the soil surface.

items.text: Residues should therefore be removed or destroyed immediately after harvest ends.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residues should therefore be removed or destroyed immediately after harvest ends.

items.text: The process includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The process includes:

items: 2. Collecting fruits left in the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. Collecting fruits left in the field.

items.text: The field should not remain for weeks after the last economically worthwhile harvest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The field should not remain for weeks after the last economically worthwhile harvest.

items.text: Field Evidence for the Importance of Residue Removal
A field study at two sites in Türkiye compared fields where tomato residues and weeds were removed and the soil cultivated with fields where residues remained without cleaning or cultivation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A field study at two sites in Türkiye compared fields where tomato residues and weeds were removed and the soil cultivated with fields where residues remained without cleaning or cultivation.

items.text: Adult moth density was higher in the uncleaned fields by approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adult moth density was higher in the uncleaned fields by approximately:

items.text: The results support combining crop-residue removal with postharvest soil cultivation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The results support combining crop-residue removal with postharvest soil cultivation.

items.text: However, the study compared a combined package of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the study compared a combined package of:

items.text: The relative effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from these results alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The relative effect of each measure cannot therefore be separated from these results alone.

items.text: Shredding Crop Residues
Fine shredding helps to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fine shredding helps to:

items: Mechanically destroy some larvae and pupae.
Facilitate incorporation into soil or transport.
Prevent intact plants from continuing as hosts.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent intact plants from continuing as hosts.

items.text: Shredding alone may not kill all pest stages and should therefore be followed by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding alone may not kill all pest stages and should therefore be followed by:

items.text: Shredding equipment should not be moved from a heavily infested field into a new field without cleaning.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Shredding equipment should not be moved from a heavily infested field into a new field without cleaning.

items.text: Tillage and Soil Cultivation
Tuta absoluta may pupate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta may pupate:

items.text: Soil cultivation after crop removal may contribute to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Soil cultivation after crop removal may contribute to:

items: Exposing others to desiccation or predation.

items.text: The effect of tillage varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The effect of tillage varies according to:

items: Ability of moths to emerge from depth.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability of moths to emerge from depth.

items.text: Tillage should not be used alone while infested plants and fruits remain on the soil surface.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tillage should not be used alone while infested plants and fruits remain on the soil surface.

items.text: Selection of Tillage Depth
No single depth is appropriate for all soils and production systems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single depth is appropriate for all soils and production systems.

items.text: Depth should be selected according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depth should be selected according to:

items.text: Some guidance recommends deep burial of infested material, but this does not imply repeated deep tillage every season because it has costs and effects on soil structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some guidance recommends deep burial of infested material, but this does not imply repeated deep tillage every season because it has costs and effects on soil structure.

items.text: The selected system should terminate host residues while maintaining soil sustainability.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The selected system should terminate host residues while maintaining soil sustainability.

items.text: Minimum Tillage and Conservation Agriculture
In conservation-agriculture systems, tillage may be reduced to protect soil structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In conservation-agriculture systems, tillage may be reduced to protect soil structure.

items.text: In this case, the absence of deep tillage should be compensated for by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In this case, the absence of deep tillage should be compensated for by:

items: Removing infested residues from the soil surface.
Managing pupae with biological methods where available.
Preventing immediate planting of a new host.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Removing infested residues from the soil surface.
[[prepared]]Managing pupae with biological methods where available.
[[prepared]]Preventing immediate planting of a new host.

items.text: Dense infested residues should not be left on the soil surface without a management plan under the justification of conserving soil.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dense infested residues should not be left on the soil surface without a management plan under the justification of conserving soil.

items.text: Solarization and Heat Treatment of Residues
Heat can be used to kill pest stages in crop residues by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Heat can be used to kill pest stages in crop residues by:

items: Placing residues in sealed bags or containers.
Exposing them to sunlight for a sufficient period.
Forming a small pile that permits temperature rise.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Placing residues in sealed bags or containers.
[[prepared]]Exposing them to sunlight for a sufficient period.
[[prepared]]Forming a small pile that permits temperature rise.

items.text: Efficacy depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy depends on:

items.text: Covering an entire open field for solarization is not a standard economical Tuta-control measure under all conditions, but heat treatment may be suitable for residues or limited areas.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Covering an entire open field for solarization is not a standard economical Tuta-control measure under all conditions, but heat treatment may be suitable for residues or limited areas.

items.text: Composting Crop Residues
Crop residues can be converted into organic compost when decomposition is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop residues can be converted into organic compost when decomposition is:

items: Able to reach temperatures lethal to insect stages.
Managed to prevent moth emergence before temperature rises.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Able to reach temperatures lethal to insect stages.
[[prepared]]Managed to prevent moth emergence before temperature rises.

items.text: An unmanaged cold residue pile may become:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An unmanaged cold residue pile may become:

items: A future source of volunteer tomato plants.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A future source of volunteer tomato plants.

items.text: Harvest Management
Regular harvesting helps to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Regular harvesting helps to:

items: Reduce persistence of ripe fruits susceptible to decay.
Reduce the time available for larvae inside fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduce persistence of ripe fruits susceptible to decay.
[[prepared]]Reduce the time available for larvae inside fruits.

items.text: During harvest:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]During harvest:

items: They should not be discarded between rows.
The area beneath the calyx should be inspected.
Contaminated crates should not be moved into new fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]They should not be discarded between rows.
[[prepared]]The area beneath the calyx should be inspected.
[[prepared]]Contaminated crates should not be moved into new fields.

items.text: Mechanical Harvesting in Processing Tomatoes
In processing tomatoes intended for once-over harvesting, the field should not reach harvest with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In processing tomatoes intended for once-over harvesting, the field should not reach harvest with:

items: A high density of rotted fruits.
A high percentage of fallen fruits.

items.text: Harvest timing depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Harvest timing depends on:

items: Preharvest interval after the final treatment.

items.text: Advancing harvest by a limited period may be economically preferable to leaving the crop until fruit infestation doubles, provided maturity percentage and factory specifications permit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advancing harvest by a limited period may be economically preferable to leaving the crop until fruit infestation doubles, provided maturity percentage and factory specifications permit.

items.text: Irrigation Management
The irrigation system does not directly kill Tuta absoluta, but it affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The irrigation system does not directly kill Tuta absoluta, but it affects:

items: Moisture and temperature of soil surrounding pupae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Moisture and temperature of soil surrounding pupae.

items.text: Balanced irrigation aims to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Balanced irrigation aims to:

items: Support the plant’s ability to compensate for limited injury.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Support the plant’s ability to compensate for limited injury.

items.text: Deliberately imposing drought should not be used as a control method because severe stress reduces yield and affects both plant tolerance and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Deliberately imposing drought should not be used as a control method because severe stress reduces yield and affects both plant tolerance and natural enemies.

items.text: Water Deficit
Studies have shown that restricted water availability can affect Tuta absoluta performance through changes in plant quality and chemical composition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have shown that restricted water availability can affect Tuta absoluta performance through changes in plant quality and chemical composition.

items.text: However, these effects are complex and may also affect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, these effects are complex and may also affect:

items.text: Laboratory results should therefore not be translated into a recommendation to impose water stress on the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Laboratory results should therefore not be translated into a recommendation to impose water stress on the crop.

items.text: The field objective remains:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The field objective remains:

items.text: Meeting the crop’s economic water requirement without excess or chronic stress.
Excess Irrigation
Excess irrigation may lead to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excess irrigation may lead to:

items: Difficulty achieving coverage with biological or chemical sprays.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Difficulty achieving coverage with biological or chemical sprays.

items.text: There is no scientific rule that increased water always increases Tuta infestation, but unbalanced growth may reduce management efficiency.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no scientific rule that increased water always increases Tuta infestation, but unbalanced growth may reduce management efficiency.

items.text: Irrigation-Water Salinity
An experimental study showed that increased salinity of irrigation water applied to tomato plants accelerated development of Tuta absoluta through plant-mediated effects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An experimental study showed that increased salinity of irrigation water applied to tomato plants accelerated development of Tuta absoluta through plant-mediated effects.

items.text: This does not mean salinity will increase pest populations to the same extent in every field, but it demonstrates that salinity is not a pest-reduction method.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean salinity will increase pest populations to the same extent in every field, but it demonstrates that salinity is not a pest-reduction method.

items.text: Salinity also causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Salinity also causes:

items: Reduced ability of the plant to compensate for injury.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduced ability of the plant to compensate for injury.

items.text: Salinity should therefore be managed according to crop requirements, with recognition that it may alter the plant–pest relationship.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Salinity should therefore be managed according to crop requirements, with recognition that it may alter the plant–pest relationship.

items.text: Fertilization Management
Fertilization indirectly affects Tuta absoluta through changes in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fertilization indirectly affects Tuta absoluta through changes in:

items: Quality of prey for predators and parasitoids.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Quality of prey for predators and parasitoids.

items.text: There is no single fertilizer program for controlling Tuta, and nutrients should not be reduced to levels that cause crop deficiency.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no single fertilizer program for controlling Tuta, and nutrients should not be reduced to levels that cause crop deficiency.

items.text: The objective is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The objective is:

items.text: Balanced nutrition that achieves the required yield without excessive vegetative growth or severe nutritional deficiency.
Nitrogen
Nitrogen is associated with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nitrogen is associated with:

items: Formation of proteins and amino acids.

items.text: Experiments have shown that nitrogen level changes larval performance, food consumption, and plant response.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Experiments have shown that nitrogen level changes larval performance, food consumption, and plant response.

items.text: However, the relationship is not a simple linear rule of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the relationship is not a simple linear rule of:

items.text: The higher the nitrogen, the greater the Tuta infestation in every case.
It is influenced by cultivar, water availability, plant defenses, and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is influenced by cultivar, water availability, plant defenses, and natural enemies.

items.text: Field management should therefore:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field management should therefore:

items: Split nitrogen applications among growth stages.
Rely on soil and plant analysis.
Balance nitrogen with potassium, calcium, and other nutrients.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Balance nitrogen with potassium, calcium, and other nutrients.

items.text: Excess Nitrogen
Excessive nitrogen may lead to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessive nitrogen may lead to:

items: Increased susceptibility to some pests and diseases.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increased susceptibility to some pests and diseases.

items.text: It may also prolong the period during which the crop remains green, extending host availability.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may also prolong the period during which the crop remains green, extending host availability.

items.text: This does not mean that reducing nitrogen below the economic requirement will profitably reduce infestation; crop yield may decline before any entomological benefit is achieved.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean that reducing nitrogen below the economic requirement will profitably reduce infestation; crop yield may decline before any entomological benefit is achieved.

items.text: Nitrogen Deficiency
Nitrogen deficiency may reduce food quality for larvae under some conditions, but it also causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nitrogen deficiency may reduce food quality for larvae under some conditions, but it also causes:

items.text: Nutritional deficiency should therefore not be used as a control measure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nutritional deficiency should therefore not be used as a control measure.

items.text: Potassium, Calcium, and Other Nutrients
Balanced nutrition contributes to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Balanced nutrition contributes to:

items.text: However, no potassium or calcium rate can be regarded as an insecticide against Tuta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, no potassium or calcium rate can be regarded as an insecticide against Tuta.

items.text: Claims that any nutrient directly eliminates larvae should be avoided unless supported by experimental evidence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Claims that any nutrient directly eliminates larvae should be avoided unless supported by experimental evidence.

items.text: Nutrients should be managed according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nutrients should be managed according to:

items.text: Organic Matter and Soil Amendments
Organic matter, compost, and vermicompost may affect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Organic matter, compost, and vermicompost may affect:

items.text: Some studies have recorded changes in pest performance on plants grown in media containing particular levels of humates or vermicompost.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some studies have recorded changes in pest performance on plants grown in media containing particular levels of humates or vermicompost.

items.text: Results vary according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results vary according to:

items.text: Soil amendments should not be relied upon as substitutes for monitoring and direct control measures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Soil amendments should not be relied upon as substitutes for monitoring and direct control measures.

items.text: Plant Density
Plant density affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant density affects:

items: Movement of larvae between touching plants.
Ability of workers to remove infestation foci.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability of workers to remove infestation foci.

items.text: Excessive density may produce an entangled canopy that is difficult to inspect and cover.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessive density may produce an entangled canopy that is difficult to inspect and cover.

items.text: Reducing density without calculation may lead to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reducing density without calculation may lead to:

items.text: Density should therefore be determined according to cultivar, training system, and mechanization, while avoiding unjustified crowding.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Density should therefore be determined according to cultivar, training system, and mechanization, while avoiding unjustified crowding.

items.text: Spacing Between Rows and Plants
Spacing should permit:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Spacing should permit:

items: Penetration of spray droplets into the canopy.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Penetration of spray droplets into the canopy.

items.text: There is no spacing specific only to Tuta, but any system that prevents access to the plant interior weakens management.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no spacing specific only to Tuta, but any system that prevents access to the plant interior weakens management.

items.text: Pruning and Leaf Removal
In indeterminate tomatoes, pruning may be used to improve ventilation and facilitate inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In indeterminate tomatoes, pruning may be used to improve ventilation and facilitate inspection.

items.text: In determinate processing tomatoes grown in open fields, extensive pruning is not usually practiced because canopy cover is necessary for fruit protection and production.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In determinate processing tomatoes grown in open fields, extensive pruning is not usually practiced because canopy cover is necessary for fruit protection and production.

items.text: Only the following should be removed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Only the following should be removed:

items: Limited infestation foci when economically justified.

items.text: Leaves close to fruit clusters should not be removed excessively in a way that exposes fruits to sunlight.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaves close to fruit clusters should not be removed excessively in a way that exposes fruits to sunlight.

items.text: Canopy Management
The desired canopy is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The desired canopy is:

items: Accessible for inspection and spray coverage.

items.text: This is achieved through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is achieved through:

items: Avoidance of excessively delayed vegetative growth.
Management of diseases that cause defoliation.

items.text: Canopy collapse caused by Tuta or diseases may expose fruits and increase sunscald and other losses.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Canopy collapse caused by Tuta or diseases may expose fruits and increase sunscald and other losses.

Tomato cultivars differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tomato cultivars differ in:

items.text: Some resistance sources in wild species are associated with glandular trichomes and chemical compounds, but commercial cultivars with complete high resistance are not broadly available for stand-alone reliance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some resistance sources in wild species are associated with glandular trichomes and chemical compounds, but commercial cultivars with complete high resistance are not broadly available for stand-alone reliance.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultivar selection should be based on:

items.text: A commercial cultivar should not be described as “Tuta-resistant” without documented comparative testing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A commercial cultivar should not be described as “Tuta-resistant” without documented comparative testing.

items.text: Intercropping
Intercropping means growing tomato with another plant in an organized pattern.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intercropping means growing tomato with another plant in an organized pattern.

items.text: It may act through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may act through:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A field study in Iran showed that intercropping tomato with sainfoin:

Reduced eggs, larvae, and mines and increased predator diversity compared with tomato monoculture.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduced eggs, larvae, and mines and increased predator diversity compared with tomato monoculture.

items.text: However, success depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, success depends on:

items.text: The system should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open-field processing tomatoes without local field trials.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The system should not be transferred directly to Egyptian open-field processing tomatoes without local field trials.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction must be made among:

items.text: Plants Supporting Natural Enemies
These provide food or shelter for parasitoids and predators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These provide food or shelter for parasitoids and predators.

items.text: Repellent Plants
These are intended to drive moths away from tomato.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are intended to drive moths away from tomato.

items.text: Trap Crops
These attract the pest away from the principal crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These attract the pest away from the principal crop.

items.text: These functions are not demonstrated merely because a plant is aromatic. They require experiments on species, distribution, and density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These functions are not demonstrated merely because a plant is aromatic. They require experiments on species, distribution, and density.

items.text: Risks of Companion Plants
Companion planting may fail or cause undesirable effects when it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Companion planting may fail or cause undesirable effects when it:

items: Competes with tomato for water and nutrients.
Becomes a weed in subsequent seasons.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Competes with tomato for water and nutrients.

items.text: A limited trial should therefore precede commercial expansion.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A limited trial should therefore precede commercial expansion.

Plant strips or belts may be established to support:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The selected plants should be:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should be planted where they do not obstruct:

items.text: Design of plants supporting natural enemies will be discussed in the section on conservation of natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Design of plants supporting natural enemies will be discussed in the section on conservation of natural enemies.

items.text: Border Crops
A solanaceous border such as potato or eggplant should not be used to attract Tuta without a management program because it may become:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A solanaceous border such as potato or eggplant should not be used to attract Tuta without a management program because it may become:

items: A source of eggs and larvae.
A focus from which moths move into tomato.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A focus from which moths move into tomato.

items.text: For a trap crop to succeed, it must:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For a trap crop to succeed, it must:

items: Be more attractive than the principal crop.
Be planted at the correct time and location.
Have pest populations removed or be destroyed before redistribution occurs.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Be more attractive than the principal crop.
[[prepared]]Be planted at the correct time and location.
[[prepared]]Have pest populations removed or be destroyed before redistribution occurs.

items.text: There is no globally standardized border crop approved for Tuta absoluta in open-field tomato.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no globally standardized border crop approved for Tuta absoluta in open-field tomato.

items.text: Management of Field Edges and Drains
Field edges and drains should be inspected for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field edges and drains should be inspected for:

items.text: Field edges should not become unmanaged areas while control is implemented only within crop rows.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field edges should not become unmanaged areas while control is implemented only within crop rows.

items.text: At the same time, non-host plants supporting natural enemies may be retained after evaluation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At the same time, non-host plants supporting natural enemies may be retained after evaluation.

Cultural control includes cleaning:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control includes cleaning:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be removed:

items: Soil and residues that may contain pupae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Soil and residues that may contain pupae.

items.text: Equipment arriving from an old field should be cleaned before entering a new field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Equipment arriving from an old field should be cleaned before entering a new field.

Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants may become centers for accumulation of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sorting stations and tomato-processing plants may become centers for accumulation of:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management requires:

Monitoring the site with traps when fields are nearby.

items.text: Coordination of Irrigation, Fertilization, and Biological Control
Plant condition affects omnivorous predators that feed on both the plant and the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant condition affects omnivorous predators that feed on both the plant and the pest.

Rather than focusing on the pest alone.

items.text: Cultural Control at the Area-Wide Level
Cultural measures are more effective when neighboring fields implement them at approximately the same time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural measures are more effective when neighboring fields implement them at approximately the same time.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Collective management includes:

items: Notification of neighboring fields before removing an infested crop.

items.text: A single field cannot prevent continuous immigration from an unmanaged production area.

Before Selecting the Land
After Selecting the Land
Before Transplanting

Do not leave crop residues between rows.

Within the shortest possible period:

Indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Indicators include:

items.text: Common Errors in Cultural Control
Error One: Considering Crop Rotation to Mean Only Changing the Tomato Cultivar
The plant host must be changed, not merely the cultivar name.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant host must be changed, not merely the cultivar name.

items.text: Error Two: Planting Potato After Tomato and Considering the Cycle Broken
Potato is a confirmed host of the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Potato is a confirmed host of the pest.

items.text: Error Three: Removing Plants but Leaving Fruits
Larvae may continue developing inside fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae may continue developing inside fruits.

items.text: Error Four: Shredding Residues and Leaving Them Untreated
Some pest stages may survive.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some pest stages may survive.

items.text: Error Five: Leaving an Old Field After Spraying Stops
It becomes an immigration source for the new crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It becomes an immigration source for the new crop.

items.text: Error Six: Removing Black Nightshade and Leaving It Along the Field Edge
Larvae may continue developing inside it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae may continue developing inside it.

items.text: Error Seven: Excessive Nitrogen Fertilization
It produces dense growth that is difficult to inspect and cover.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It produces dense growth that is difficult to inspect and cover.

items.text: Error Eight: Imposing Water Stress to Control Tuta
It damages the crop and natural enemies and is not a reliable control measure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It damages the crop and natural enemies and is not a reliable control measure.

items.text: Error Nine: Relying on Salinity to Weaken the Pest
Salinity may accelerate pest development and weaken the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Salinity may accelerate pest development and weaken the crop.

items.text: Error Ten: Removing Too Many Leaves
This exposes fruits to sunlight and reduces photosynthesis.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This exposes fruits to sunlight and reduces photosynthesis.

items.text: Error Eleven: Planting an Aromatic Species and Assuming It Is a Proven Repellent
Companion plants require local scientific testing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Companion plants require local scientific testing.

items.text: Error Twelve: Applying Sanitation in Only One Field
Immigration may continue from neighboring fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Immigration may continue from neighboring fields.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Discarding Sorting Rejects Near Fields
They become sources of larvae and volunteer tomato plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They become sources of larvae and volunteer tomato plants.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Randomly Burning Residues
This causes pollution and hazards and should be used only in accordance with regulations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This causes pollution and hazards and should be used only in accordance with regulations.

items.text: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
The principal priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal priorities are:

items: 1. Remove old fields immediately after harvesting ceases to be economically worthwhile.
2. Do not stop control while leaving a live field beside a new planting.
3. Remove volunteer tomato plants around fields and processing facilities.
4. Monitor black nightshade and solanaceous hosts.
5. Prevent continuous succession of tomato, potato, and eggplant without a break.
6. Collect infested fruits and do not discard them between rows.
7. Shred and treat tomato residues immediately after harvest.
8. Use traps to monitor moth emergence from old fields.
9. Regulate nitrogen and irrigation according to actual crop requirements.
10. Preserve canopy cover in processing tomatoes.
12. Manage waste from tomato-processing plants and sorting stations.
13. Coordinate measures among growers within the same production area.
14. Test supportive plants or intercropping on a limited scale before expansion.
15. Evaluate measures according to reductions in fruit infestation and spray number, not merely the visual cleanliness of the field.

[[prepared]]2. Do not stop control while leaving a live field beside a new planting.
[[prepared]]3. Remove volunteer tomato plants around fields and processing facilities.
[[prepared]]4. Monitor black nightshade and solanaceous hosts.
[[prepared]]5. Prevent continuous succession of tomato, potato, and eggplant without a break.
[[prepared]]6. Collect infested fruits and do not discard them between rows.
[[prepared]]7. Shred and treat tomato residues immediately after harvest.
[[prepared]]8. Use traps to monitor moth emergence from old fields.
[[prepared]]9. Regulate nitrogen and irrigation according to actual crop requirements.
[[prepared]]10. Preserve canopy cover in processing tomatoes.
[[prepared]]12. Manage waste from tomato-processing plants and sorting stations.
[[prepared]]13. Coordinate measures among growers within the same production area.
[[prepared]]14. Test supportive plants or intercropping on a limited scale before expansion.
[[prepared]]15. Evaluate measures according to reductions in fruit infestation and spray number, not merely the visual cleanliness of the field.

items.text: Table of Cultural-Control Components
Summary of Section Five
Cultural control reduces the ability of Tuta absoluta to reach suitable hosts, complete its life cycle, and move among planting seasons.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control reduces the ability of Tuta absoluta to reach suitable hosts, complete its life cycle, and move among planting seasons.

items.text: Its principal foundations include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its principal foundations include:

items: Management of black nightshade and alternative hosts.
Removal of infested plant parts and fruits when economically justified.
Removal and treatment of crop residues.
Maintenance of suitable plant density and canopy cover.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Management of black nightshade and alternative hosts.
[[prepared]]Removal of infested plant parts and fruits when economically justified.
[[prepared]]Maintenance of suitable plant density and canopy cover.

items.text: Crop-residue removal is among the most important measures. A field study recorded moth populations approximately 47.77% and 73.97% higher in fields that were not cleaned or cultivated after harvest than in cleaned and cultivated fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop-residue removal is among the most important measures. A field study recorded moth populations approximately 47.77% and 73.97% higher in fields that were not cleaned or cultivated after harvest than in cleaned and cultivated fields.

items.text: The effect of tillage cannot be separated from residue removal in that study, so the two measures should be viewed as a combined package.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The effect of tillage cannot be separated from residue removal in that study, so the two measures should be viewed as a combined package.

items.text: Irrigation and fertilization affect interactions among the plant, pest, and natural enemies, but water stress or nutrient deficiency should not be used as direct control methods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Irrigation and fertilization affect interactions among the plant, pest, and natural enemies, but water stress or nutrient deficiency should not be used as direct control methods.

items.text: Intercropping and supportive plants may contribute to reducing pest pressure and increasing natural enemies, but they require local calibration before adoption in open-field processing-tomato systems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intercropping and supportive plants may contribute to reducing pest pressure and increasing natural enemies, but they require local calibration before adoption in open-field processing-tomato systems.

items.text: Cultural control cannot be completely successful in one field when surrounding old fields, crop residues, and alternative hosts remain continuous immigration sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cultural control cannot be completely successful in one field when surrounding old fields, crop residues, and alternative hosts remain continuous immigration sources.
